discussion_comments.comment_body:
we need to explicitly check for `undefined` here because it’s the only indicator for “empty” we have. `null` is technically allowed:

```suggestion
          reducer(prev === undefined ? initialValue : prev, chunk),
```
this changes makes sure we never resolve with `undefined` for imperative methods like `queryClient.fetchQuery(...)`. The bang operator `!` was a dead giveaway that this could happen 😅

discussion_comments.comment_body: a truthy check for `query.state.data` is not enough because `null`, `false` or `""` can all be valid values for caching a resolved query. We need to explicitly check against `undefined` please.
oh also - this is missing the check for `revalidateIfStale` option 😅
A yes of course, will add that!

this isn't ideal because a resolved value can and should never be `undefined`. Instead, I guess we should set the `queryFn` itself back to `undefined`, or to a function that throws (maybe in dev mode with a descriptive message?), in `defaultQueryOptions` when we set `enabled: false`.

Usually, the queryFn won't run, but if you pass a `skipToken` and then call `refetch` manually - it would, but that's kind of an invariant
Sounds good, on it

+import { hashKey } from '@tanstack/query-core'
+import type {
+  QueryClient,
+  QueryFunctionContext,
+  QueryKey,
+  QueryState,
+} from '@tanstack/query-core'
+
+export interface PersistedQuery {
+  buster: string
+  timestamp: number
+  queryHash: string
+  queryKey: QueryKey
+  state: QueryState
+}
+
+export interface AsyncStorage {
+  getItem: (key: string) => Promise<string | undefined | null>
+  setItem: (key: string, value: string) => Promise<void>
+  removeItem: (key: string) => Promise<void>
+}
+
+export interface StoragePersisterOptions<QC extends QueryClient> {
+  /**
+   * Query Client instance
+   */
+  queryClient: QC
+  /** The storage client used for setting and retrieving items from cache.
+   * For SSR pass in `undefined`.
+   */
+  storage: AsyncStorage | Storage | undefined | null
+  /**
+   * How to serialize the data to storage.
+   * @default `JSON.stringify`
+   */
+  serialize?: (client: PersistedQuery) => string
+  /**
+   * How to deserialize the data from storage.
+   * @default `JSON.parse`
+   */
+  deserialize?: (cachedString: string) => PersistedQuery
+  /**
+   * A unique string that can be used to forcefully invalidate existing caches,
+   * if they do not share the same buster string
+   */
+  buster?: string
+  /**
+   * The max-allowed age of the cache in milliseconds.
+   * If a persisted cache is found that is older than this
+   * time, it will be discarded
+   */
+  maxAge?: number
+}
+
+/**
+ * Warning: experimental feature.
+ * This utility function enables fine-grained query persistance.
+ * Simple add it as a `persister` parameter to `useQuery` or `defaultOptions` on `queryClient`.
+ * 
+ * ```
+ * useQuery({
+     queryKey: ['myKey'],
+     queryFn: fetcher,
+     persister: createPersister({
+       storage: localStorage,
+       queryClient,
+     }),
+   })
+   ```
+ */
+export function createPersister<T, QC extends QueryClient>({
+  queryClient,
+  storage,
+  buster = '',
+  maxAge = 1000 * 60 * 60 * 24,
+  serialize = JSON.stringify,
+  deserialize = JSON.parse,
+}: StoragePersisterOptions<QC>) {

+    queryFn: (context: QueryFunctionContext) => T | Promise<T>,

+  ) => {
+    const queryHash = hashKey(context.queryKey)
+    const queryState = queryClient.getQueryState(context.queryKey)
+
+    // Try to restore only if we do not have any data in the cache and we have persister defined
+    if (!queryState?.data && storage != null) {

discussion_comments.comment_body: I think we need to check for `queryState?.data !== undefined` explicitly, because having falsy values in `data` for a successful query is a legit use-case.

discussion_comments.comment_body:
This actually has a different meaning, it _only_ prevents the value `undefined` by itself. `undefined extends T` passes if the generic union contains `undefined`. 


I think they'll both correctly cause compile errors, but `undefined extends T` seems like it gives a clearer error message closer to the source. 

`T extends undefined`
![image](https://github.com/TanStack/query/assets/5288805/a477c9f8-a18d-499e-8de1-edb392b18b29)
`undefined extends T`
![image](https://github.com/TanStack/query/assets/5288805/7235502a-7a81-4a2f-93cd-2c8d8ea6bf3c)
 


See: https://www.typescriptlang.org/play?#code/C4TwDgpgBAKgogD2BAdgEwM4FV0QGYCWKEaAPDAHxQC8sUESqmUArrocWlAPxTEBuEAE5QAXLADcAKFCQoADRqxEydNnZESpFCwC2AI2FQAPqw2cK0gPRWodgHrcpM8NBxp8mtCqYYY5Klo2Dw4SekY1Ol4BI3EYaVloAE0ld09OHzV-HQMjU2D0kkspGzsoRyA

But curious for your thoughts! (maybe we should be switching that existing implementation too?)

discussion_comments.comment_body:
Okay @TkDodo it should be hopefully fixed with 4eba53bd6ee78cc2fe50fe7223d5b2fc506aaf3d -- also added tests about `null` return values + initialState. 

Couple of notes:
- I honestly don't understand how the fix worked...

```ts
unknown extends T ? unknown : undefined extends T ? never : T | Promise<T>
// vs
unknown extends T ? T : undefined extends T ? never : T | Promise<T>
```
- I think(?) it made the error messages slightly worse 

![image](https://github.com/TanStack/query/assets/5288805/d4b52538-4c60-4f81-9dfd-d86cf8d727ea)
- It also means that `data` ends up being inferred with the types, including `undefined` -- instead of `unknown` as it was previously. (because of this, I removed the type `Expect<>` and just left the `@ts-expect-error` annotations)

![image](https://github.com/TanStack/query/assets/5288805/6700b35a-caf0-4b04-b380-97e92a1e6e4c)




Regardless of all of this weirdness, I still 100% think this is better than the existing state + avoids some nasty/unexpected runtime issues. But would also appreciate another quick smoketest from your side too!

discussion_comments.comment_body: Updated the code as requested based on the review comment.
same here: `fetchStatus` is not a boolean, we just need to check if it was passed
why can’t we continue to use `noop` here ?
If we use noop(function noop(): void {}, not function noop(): undefined {}), TypeScript error will be occured
at runtime, there’s no difference between a function that returns `undefined` and a function that returns `void`. would be good if we could keep `noop` here
